summary: Tooling PR that changed convert_data.py to emit `.asciz` for detected zero-terminated strings instead of mixing `.ascii` with separate `NULL` dwords. The stated goal was to remove false positives and hanging `NULL` bytes. The generated asm updates in sysdolphin baselib mostly replace `.ascii` strings plus explicit NULL/alignment with `.asciz` plus `.balign 4`, while some printable-looking non-string byte runs are restored to `.4byte` data. The PR was closed unmerged and explicitly superseded by #462.
what_changed: Renamed the string-detection path from ASCII terminology to ASCIZ terminology: `find_ascii` became `find_asciz`, `min_ascii_len` became `min_asciz_len`, and `try_get_ascii` became `try_get_asciz`.
Changed detected zero-terminated strings to be emitted as `.asciz "..."`, with `.balign 4` added when padding is needed.

Adjusted string detection so the zero terminator is included in the consumed length, and any required alignment bytes after the terminator must actually be zero before accepting the buffer as an asciz string.
Changed zero handling in `convert_data.py`: `zero` default became `None`, `None` maps to `.4byte NULL`, and the previous explicit `'null'` option was removed.
Made `try_get_zero` always part of the conversion attempt list instead of only being used when a zero option was truthy.
Changed the tool input from one fixed file, `asm/sysdolphin/baselib/baselib_unknown_001.s`, to a glob over `asm/sysdolphin/baselib/baselib_unknown_*.s`.
Regenerated baselib unknown asm data: many `.ascii` string directives became `.asciz`, explicit `.4byte NULL` terminators were removed, and `.balign 4` was inserted where necessary.

Some sequences that previously looked printable enough to become `.ascii`, such as `Qhq\\1@NWgyxeH\\_bpdgc`, `cccc/B...`, and `5<=6/7>?`, were converted back to raw `.4byte` values, consistent with the PR goal of reducing false positives.
smart_moves: The tool no longer represents a C-style string as two separate concepts (`.ascii` plus a trailing `NULL` dword); `.asciz` encodes the terminator directly, making the asm intent clearer.
Requiring post-terminator alignment bytes to be zero before accepting an asciz candidate is a useful guard against treating arbitrary printable byte arrays as strings.
Keeping `.balign 4` separate from `.asciz` preserves section alignment without leaving misleading standalone `NULL` dwords after strings.
Running the converter over a glob of baselib unknown files let the same data-format rule be applied consistently to both affected baselib asm files.
Renaming code and options from `ascii` to `asciz` documents the stricter invariant: the detected data must be a null-terminated string, not merely printable bytes.
decomp_lessons: For decomp asm data, prefer `.asciz` for known null-terminated strings rather than `.ascii` followed by `.4byte NULL`; it is more semantically accurate and avoids hanging terminator artifacts.
Printable bytes alone are weak evidence for a string. Require a real zero terminator and valid zero padding/alignment before converting raw data into a string directive.
After `.asciz`, use `.balign 4` when needed to preserve object layout; do not leave explicit terminator dwords unless the binary data actually represents separate data.
Automated data converters should be conservative: false-positive strings can obscure arrays/tables and make later decompilation harder.
When changing a data-conversion heuristic, regenerate representative asm outputs to expose both intended string conversions and rejected false positives.
naming_conventions: `asciz` was used in tool option/function names when the heuristic specifically detects zero-terminated strings: `find_asciz`, `min_asciz_len`, `try_get_asciz`.
The PR title and diff consistently use assembler directive spelling `.asciz` for null-terminated strings.
Generic label names such as `lbl_803B9670`, `lbl_8040ADC4`, and `jtbl_8040A7A8` were not renamed in this PR; the change was representation/tooling only.
`input_file` became `input_glob` to reflect processing multiple matching asm files.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Replace `.ascii "string"` followed by `.4byte NULL` with `.asciz "string"` and then `.balign 4` if four-byte alignment is required.
If a printable-looking byte sequence lacks a valid null terminator plus zero padding, keep it as explicit `.4byte 0x...` data rather than a string directive.
Include the null terminator in the consumed length for string detection, then round up with `ceil(length / 4) * 4` to calculate alignment.
Escape backslashes and newlines when emitting string text, as shown by the conversion code's `.replace('\\', '\\\\').replace('\n', '\\n')` pattern.
Continue parsing `NULL` input as zero bytes when reading existing asm, independent of the output formatting option.
review_feedback: No review comments were present in the slice.
The PR was closed with `merged_at` null, so future researchers should consult PR #462 for the accepted successor if available.
follow_up_queries: Inspect PR #462 to see the final accepted version of the asciz conversion heuristic.
Search for remaining `.ascii` plus `.4byte NULL` patterns that should be represented as `.asciz`.
Audit printable `.4byte` regions before converting them to strings; confirm null termination and padding.
Check whether other data-conversion scripts use `ascii` terminology for null-terminated strings and should adopt `asciz` naming.
